does: This paper identifies and systematically characterizes termination poisoning attacks, where adversaries inject malicious prompts into an LLM agent's context to corrupt its task-completion judgment, causing unbounded loops of repeated reasoning and action. The authors define this threat formally, design 10 representative attack strategies targeting the self-evaluation mechanisms that determine when agents decide tasks are finished, and conduct extensive empirical evaluation across 8 different LLM agents and 60 tasks. They discover that different agents exhibit distinct behavioral signatures determining attack success, revealing transferable patterns in vulnerability that enable principled crafting of attacks against unseen agents and tasks. The work culminates in LoopTrap, an automated red-teaming framework that leverages these behavioral patterns to scale attack discovery beyond manual templates and systematically probe agent robustness.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial attacks against LLM agents, building on prior research into prompt injection vulnerabilities and jailbreaking techniques. It connects to broader studies of agent reliability and safety that examine failure modes in iterative reasoning systems, particularly work investigating how agents verify task completion and manage execution loops. The paper responds to increasing concerns about autonomous LLM systems operating without human oversight, synthesizing insights from red-teaming methodologies and adversarial robustness evaluation. It builds on foundational work in understanding how language models can be manipulated through context poisoning, adapting these insights to the specific architectural pattern of self-directed agentic loops where termination logic becomes a critical attack surface.
enables: This research enables more robust design of LLM agent architectures by identifying specific mechanisms vulnerable to termination corruption, potentially spurring development of hardened termination criteria and verification procedures. The transferable attack patterns and LoopTrap framework establish systematic methods for red-teaming autonomous agents, facilitating proactive identification of vulnerabilities in deployed systems. The work opens directions for developing defenses such as external verification mechanisms, robust termination oracles, and adversarial training approaches. More broadly, it highlights the need for safety mechanisms that prevent unbounded computation in agentic loops, motivating research into certified termination properties and formal verification of agent behavior, while establishing benchmarks for evaluating agent robustness against context-based adversarial attacks at scale.
